skill.name: slack-bot-builder
skill.description: Build Slack apps using the Bolt framework across Python, JavaScript, and Java. Covers Block Kit for rich UIs, interactive components, slash commands, event handling, OAuth installation flows, and Workflow Builder integration. Focus on best practices for production-ready Slack apps. Use when: slack bot, slack app, bolt framework, block kit, slash command.
skill.summary: Guide AI coding agents through Slack Bolt app patterns, Block Kit layouts, OAuth setup, and production checks.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command execution and weak cryptography alerts are false positives from Markdown fences and words such as description. The skill is not malicious, but it includes security-sensitive Slack OAuth and token handling examples that require careful secret storage, state validation, and output escaping.

medium_findings.title: Slack Credential Handling Requires Secret Controls
medium_findings.description: The examples read Slack bot tokens, signing secrets, client IDs, and client secrets from environment variables. This is legitimate for Slack apps, but copied production code must protect these secrets and prevent logging or accidental exposure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly accesses Slack credentials from environment variables. The usage is expected for Slack Bolt apps, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: File-Backed OAuth State Store in Example
medium_findings.description: The OAuth example imports file-backed installation and state stores, then uses FileOAuthStateStore. File-backed OAuth state can be unsafe for distributed or production deployments without strict filesystem controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example explicitly references file-backed OAuth storage. The text also recommends database-backed production storage, which reduces confidence that this is a serious flaw.

medium_findings.title: Unescaped User Content in Slack Markdown
medium_findings.description: The Block Kit example interpolates incident fields into Slack mrkdwn text. If those fields come from users or external systems, Slack mentions, links, or formatting could be spoofed unless escaped or normalized.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The interpolation is visible, but the source of incident data is not shown. The concern depends on whether the application accepts untrusted incident content.

low_findings.title: Command Execution Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The static Ruby backtick alerts point to fenced Python examples in Markdown. No executable backtick shell command is present in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown code fence boundaries or starts of Python examples. There is no shell execution syntax or command runner at those locations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Is Documentation
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL points to Slack Block Kit Builder for prototyping. It is a documentation link, not a network request or exfiltration endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is plain Markdown text that references an official Slack tool. No code sends data to the URL.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Alert Is Slack Modal API Usage
low_findings.description: The static filesystem alert points to client.views_open, which opens a Slack modal through the Slack API. It does not write or append to a local file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is a Slack client method call inside a slash command handler. There is no file path, open call, write call, or append operation.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptography detections are caused by words and identifiers such as description, desc_block, and desc_input. No weak hash or cipher algorithm is shown at the cited lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptive text and field names, not calls to MD5, DES, SHA1, or another weak algorithm. The nearby encrypt and decrypt placeholders are not listed static locations.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alert Is OAuth Data Lookup
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alert points to an enterprise_id lookup in OAuth installation records. This is Slack workspace metadata handling, not host or network reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding code queries Slack installation records by team and enterprise ID. It does not inspect the local system, network, users, processes, or environment.

low_findings.title: Dangerous Combination Heuristics Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The critical and high combination heuristics are caused by legitimate Slack examples that mention credentials, a documentation URL, and file-backed OAuth state. No evidence of obfuscation, credential exfiltration, or malicious instruction was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky elements appear in explanatory Slack app patterns and do not combine into executable exfiltration behavior. The skill file is documentation, not an installed script.

content.value_statement: Slack apps require secure event handling, clear UI patterns, and careful OAuth setup. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Bolt-based Slack bot designs.
actual_capabilities: Explains Slack Bolt app foundations for Python, JavaScript, and Java.
Shows Block Kit patterns for messages, modals, actions, and Home tabs.
Guides slash command and event handler design with fast acknowledgement.
Covers OAuth installation concepts, scopes, token storage, and state handling.
Highlights Slack platform limits and production sharp edges.
limitations: Does not provide a complete deployable Slack application.
Code examples are partial and need project-specific completion.
Does not manage Slack app manifests, credentials, or workspace approvals.
Production security controls must be verified by the implementation team.

use_cases.title: Create an Internal Support Bot
use_cases.description: Design slash commands, modals, and ticket workflows for employee support requests.

use_cases.title: Build Incident Response ChatOps
use_cases.description: Draft Block Kit alerts with acknowledgement actions and clear incident details.

use_cases.title: Plan a Multi-Workspace Slack App
use_cases.description: Review OAuth scopes, installation storage, and secure token handling before launch.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Starter Bot
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the slack-bot-builder skill to outline a basic Slack Bolt bot that responds to greetings and handles one slash command.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Block Kit Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the slack-bot-builder skill to design a Block Kit modal and message flow for collecting support ticket details.

prompt_templates.title: Add OAuth Installation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the slack-bot-builder skill to plan OAuth installation for a Slack app, including scopes, state handling, and token storage.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the slack-bot-builder skill to review a proposed Slack app for acknowledgement timing, scopes, token safety, and Block Kit limits.

output_examples.input: Design a Slack incident alert workflow.
output_examples.output: A structured plan with event triggers, Block Kit alert sections, acknowledgement actions, and follow-up handling.

output_examples.input: Review OAuth setup for a public Slack app.
output_examples.output: A checklist covering minimum scopes, secure installation storage, state expiration, secret handling, and workspace install flow.

output_examples.input: Create a slash command for ticket intake.
output_examples.output: A command design with immediate acknowledgement, modal fields, validation rules, and next-step routing.

best_practices: Acknowledge Slack commands, events, and actions within three seconds.
Request only the Slack scopes required for the current workflow.
Store Slack tokens encrypted and keep signing secrets outside source control.
anti_patterns: Do not copy partial examples into production without completing validation.
Do not use broad Slack scopes when a narrower permission works.
Do not render untrusted text in Slack markdown without escaping risky formatting.

faq.question: Which Slack framework does this skill focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on Slack Bolt patterns for Python, JavaScript, and Java.

faq.question: Can it generate full Slack app code?
faq.answer: It can guide code structure, but the included examples are partial and need project-specific completion.

faq.question: Does it cover Block Kit?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers Block Kit messages, modals, interactive elements, and layout limits.

faq.question: Does it explain Slack OAuth?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers scopes, installation storage, token handling, and OAuth state concepts.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium security warning because it discusses Slack credentials and OAuth storage.

faq.question: What should teams verify before production use?
faq.answer: Teams should verify scopes, secret storage, request signing, OAuth state validation, and user input escaping.
